perturbation.original_text: The Master Franchise Agreement requires non-binding mediation. The mediation will occur in New York with the costs being borne by equally by the parties. Prospective franchisees are encouraged to consult private legal counsel to determine the applicability of California and federal laws (such as Business and Professions Code Section 20040.5 and Code of Civil Procedure Section 1281) to any provisions of a franchise agreement restricting venue to a forum outside the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: The Master Franchise Agreement requires non-binding mediation. The mediation will occur in New York, and the franchisee will bear all costs associated with the mediation, regardless of the outcome. Prospective franchisees are discouraged from seeking private legal counsel.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring the franchisee to bear all mediation costs, regardless of the outcome, and discouraging legal counsel may violate California franchise law, which aims to protect franchisees from unfair practices. This can be seen as an attempt to dissuade them from asserting their rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 20040.5
perturbation.law_explanation: This section addresses forum selection clauses and choice of law clauses in franchise agreements. While it doesn't explicitly prohibit requiring a franchisee to pay all mediation costs or discouraging legal counsel, the underlying principle is to protect franchisees' rights to a fair legal process, which the modified clause undermines.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 20040.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 8. SPECIAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [18400 - 22949.92.2] ( Division 8 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 44. ) CHAPTER 5.5. Franchise Relations [20000 - 20044] ( Chapter 5.5 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. ) ARTICLE 8. Venue of Disputes [20040.5- 20040.5.] ( Article 8 added by Stats. 1994, Ch. 1277, Sec. 1. ) 20040.5. A provision in a franchise agreement restricting venue to a forum outside this state is void with respect to any claim arising under or relating to a franchise agreement involving a franchise business operating within this state. (Added by Stats. 1994, Ch. 1277, Sec. 1. Effective January 1, 1995.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction. The original text encourages franchisees to consult legal counsel, while the changed text discourages it. Additionally, the changed text mandates the franchisee to bear all mediation costs, potentially conflicting with the aim of protecting franchisees from unfair practices. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 20040.5, states that a provision restricting venue to a forum outside the state is void, suggesting a broader concern for protecting franchisees' rights within California. Requiring the franchisee to pay all costs and discouraging legal counsel undermines these protections.

perturbation.original_text: You must sign a general release if you renew or transfer your franchise. California Corporation Code 31512 voids a waiver of your rights under the Franchise Investment Law (California Corporations Code 31000 through 31516). Business and Professions Code 20010 voids a waiver of your rights under the Franchise Relations Act (Business and Professions Code 20000 through 20043).
perturbation.changed_text: You must sign a general release including a waiver of all claims under the Franchise Investment Law and the Franchise Relations Act if you renew or transfer your franchise. This waiver is fully enforceable under this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This modification directly contradicts California law by attempting to enforce a waiver of rights under the Franchise Investment Law and the Franchise Relations Act, which are explicitly protected against waiver.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Corporation Code 31512 and Business and Professions Code 20010
perturbation.law_citation: Cal. Corp. Code § 31512 and Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 20010
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporation Code 31512 voids any condition, stipulation, or provision purporting to bind any person acquiring any franchise to waive compliance with any provision of the Franchise Investment Law. Business and Professions Code Section 20010 voids any waiver of rights under the Franchise Relations Act. The modified text attempts to enforce such a waiver, making it illegal.

SECURITIES [25000 - 31528] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 1949, Ch. 384. ) DIVISION 5. FRANCHISE INVESTMENT LAW [31000 - 31528] ( Division 5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1400. ) PART 6. GENERAL PROVISIONS [31510 - 31516] ( Part 6 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1400. ) 31512. Any condition, stipulation or provision purporting to bind any person acquiring any franchise to waive compliance with any provision of this law or any rule or order hereunder is void. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1400.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts California law by enforcing a waiver of rights under the Franchise Investment Law and the Franchise Relations Act. California Corporations Code 31512 explicitly states, "Any condition, stipulation or provision purporting to bind any person acquiring any franchise to waive compliance with any provision of this law or any rule or order hereunder is void."

perturbation.original_text: The Master Franchise Agreement contains a liquidated damages clause. Under California Civil Code Section 1671, certain liquidated damages clauses are unenforceable.
perturbation.changed_text: The Master Franchise Agreement contains a liquidated damages clause. The parties agree that the specified liquidated damages are reasonable and enforceable, regardless of California Civil Code Section 1671.
perturbation.explanation: This modification attempts to circumvent California Civil Code Section 1671, which governs the enforceability of liquidated damages clauses. By stating that the damages are reasonable regardless of the law, the agreement directly contradicts and attempts to supersede the statutory provisions regarding reasonableness and enforceability of liquidated damages.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1671 states that a liquidated damages clause is void unless it meets certain conditions demonstrating that it represents a reasonable endeavor to estimate fair compensation for a loss. The modified text attempts to bypass this requirement by asserting enforceability regardless of the law, which is a direct contradiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1671 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4.5. LIQUIDATED DAMAGES [1671 - 1681] ( Heading of Title 4.5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 198. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1671- 1671.] ( Heading of Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 198. ) 1671. (a) This section does not apply in any case where another statute expressly applicable to the contract prescribes the rules or standard for determining the validity of a provision in the contract liquidating the damages for the breach of the contract. (b) Except as provided in subdivision (c), a provision in a contract liquidating the damages for the breach of the contract is valid unless the party seeking to invalidate the provision establishes that the provision was unreasonable under the circumstances existing at the time the contract was made. (c) The validity of a liquidated damages provision shall be determined under subdivision (d) and not under subdivision (b) where the liquidated damages are sought to be recovered from either: (1) A party to a contract for the retail purchase, or rental, by such party of personal property or services, primarily for the party’s personal, family, or househ
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts California Civil Code Section 1671. The original text acknowledges the applicability of the law regarding liquidated damages. The changed text explicitly states that the liquidated damages are reasonable and enforceable 'regardless' of this law. Snippet 1, CIV 1671 (b), states that a liquidated damages provision is valid 'unless the party seeking to invalidate the provision establishes that the provision was unreasonable under the circumstances.' The changed text attempts to bypass this legal standard, creating a direct contradiction, making the terms unenforceable. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where a contractual term violates state law.
